title: Obsidian-style Graph View
problem: The tree view shows lineage as an indented list — useful for reading in order, but it hides the topology. Readers cannot see the full web of connections at a glance: which features are densely connected, which are isolated, which form clusters. A force-directed graph would let any visitor instantly grasp the shape of the decision space.
analysis: D3 force simulation positions nodes by repulsion + spring forces along parent/child edges. Each node is a feature; edges are parent→child relationships. Clicking a node triggers the same focus mechanism as the tree view. The graph shares the app's existing featureKey/focus state so both views stay in sync. The warm archival palette maps status colours to node fills; node size encodes completeness_pct.

decisions.decision: Reuse the existing focusKey state rather than a separate graph-selection concept
decisions.rationale: The app already has a working focus mechanism with URL sync and breadcrumb navigation. Sharing it between views means clicking a node in the graph and then switching to the tree view highlights the same feature — the views compose naturally.
alternativesConsidered: Separate selectedKey for graph — would need its own URL param and breadcrumb integration, duplicating logic

decisions.decision: Dark warm background for the graph canvas (#1C1714)
decisions.rationale: The Obsidian graph idiom reads best against a dark background. Using the --text colour (very dark warm brown) preserves the archival palette rather than reaching for a generic #0d1117.
alternativesConsidered: Light cream background — nodes on cream washed out and edges were invisible
Pure black — works but breaks the warm aesthetic

decisions.decision: View toggle (tree / graph) in the section header, not a separate route
decisions.rationale: The app has no router. A toggle button is the minimal change; adding routing would require restructuring the entire app for one mode switch.

lineage.spawnReason: The tree view (feat-2026-005) established collapsible lineage threads; the next natural step is a spatial graph view so visitors can see the full topology at once rather than reading the tree linearly.

implementation: FeatureGraph.tsx uses d3 force simulation (forceLink + forceManyBody + forceCenter + forceCollide) rendered into an SVG. Nodes are coloured by status using CSS custom property values; node radius scales from 5–14px based on completeness_pct. Edges connect parent→child. Hover highlights the hovered node's edges and fades unrelated nodes. Clicking a node calls onNodeClick(featureKey) which sets the App's focusKey — the same handler used by the tree view's focus button. Pan and zoom are handled by d3-zoom on the SVG element.
successCriteria: A 'Graph' toggle appears next to the feature list heading. Clicking it replaces the tree with a force-directed graph. Nodes are draggable. Hovering a node highlights its edges. Clicking a node focuses it (same breadcrumb behaviour as the tree). Clicking 'Tree' returns to the list.
knownLimitations: No ResizeObserver — graph dimensions are captured once at mount; resizing the browser window or toggling the sidebar won't reflow the simulation without a page reload
features.find() is called on every D3 mousemove/hover event — O(n) linear scan per frame; should be a pre-built Map<string, Feature> for performance at scale
completeness_pct is not a field on the Feature type, so all nodes default to 50 via ?? 50 — every node renders at the same radius regardless of actual documentation completeness
Node labels are only visible on hover (tooltip) — the graph has no always-visible text, so features aren't identifiable at a glance without mousing over each node individually
